flashcards.front: Define Depth First Search (DFS) formally and state its key characteristic.
flashcards.back: DFS is a graph traversal algorithm that explores vertices by going as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking. Given G=(V,E) and source s∈V, DFS explores all vertices reachable from s by maintaining a LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) frontier that prioritizes depth over breadth. The DFS forest preserves parent-child relationships from the discovery edge.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of DFS using adjacency list representation? Derive it.
flashcards.back: O(V + E) time complexity. Each vertex is visited exactly once (O(V)), and every edge in the adjacency list is examined exactly once during exploration of its incident vertices (O(E)). Total: Θ(V + E). For adjacency matrix, it's O(V²) since we must check all V vertices for each vertex's adjacency check.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of DFS? Explain the components.
flashcards.back: O(V) space complexity. This includes: (1) Visited/color array: O(V), (2) Discovery time array: O(V), (3) Finish time array: O(V), (4) Parent array: O(V), (5) Recursion stack (or explicit stack) storing at most O(V) vertices in worst case (a linear chain graph). Total: Θ(V).

flashcards.front: Classify the four types of edges in a DFS forest with definitions.
flashcards.back: (1) Tree Edge: Edge to an unvisited vertex; forms the DFS tree. (2) Back Edge: Edge to an ancestor in DFS tree; indicates cycle in directed graph. (3) Forward Edge: Edge to a descendant in DFS tree (non-tree edge). (4) Cross Edge: Edge between vertices where neither is ancestor of the other. In undirected graphs, only tree edges and back edges exist (forward/cross appear as back to ancestor).

flashcards.front: State and prove the Parenthesis Theorem of DFS.
flashcards.back: Theorem: For any two vertices u and v, the intervals [d[u], f[u]] and [d[v], f[v]] are either disjoint or one is contained within the other. Proof: If u is ancestor of v, then DFS discovers u before v, so d[u] < d[v]. v is fully explored before backtracking from u, so f[v] < f[u]. Thus [d[v], f[v]] ⊂ [d[u], f[u]]. If neither is ancestor, exploration of one completes before the other begins, so intervals are disjoint.

flashcards.front: How does DFS detect cycles in a directed graph?
flashcards.back: A directed graph contains a cycle if and only if DFS yields a back edge. During DFS, when exploring vertex u, if we encounter a vertex v that is currently in the recursion stack (has color GRAY), then edge (u,v) is a back edge forming a cycle. The cycle consists of v and its ancestors down to u in the DFS tree.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between DFS on directed vs undirected graphs for cycle detection?
flashcards.back: Undirected: Cycle exists if an explored edge connects to an already-visited (WHITE) vertex that is not the parent. No need for color check since undirected edges appear twice. Directed: Must use coloring—cycle exists only if back edge found to GRAY (ancestor) vertex. Edge to BLACK (already finished) vertex does not indicate cycle.

flashcards.front: Explain the white-path theorem and its significance.
flashcards.back: Theorem: Vertex v is a descendant of u in DFS forest iff at time d[u], there exists a path from u to v consisting entirely of white vertices. Significance: Used to prove correctness of edge classification; establishes that discovery order determines ancestor-descendant relationships in DFS tree.

flashcards.front: What is the role of timestamps (discovery and finish times) in DFS?
flashcards.back: Timestamps track DFS execution: d[v] = time when v is first discovered (colored GRAY), f[v] = time when all adjacent vertices are finished (colored BLACK). They form the DFS numbering system used in parenthesis theorem, edge classification, and applications like topological sort and strongly connected components. Total timestamps range from 1 to 2|V|.

flashcards.front: Compare recursive DFS vs iterative DFS implementation.
flashcards.back: Recursive: Uses system call stack implicitly, cleaner code, but risk of stack overflow for deep graphs (V > 10⁴). Iterative: Uses explicit stack, avoids stack overflow, same time complexity, but slightly more complex code to manage visited state. Both produce same discovery/finish times and edge classification under identical tie-breaking rules.

flashcards.front: How can DFS be used to find strongly connected components?
flashcards.back: Strongly Connected Components (SCCs) are found using Kosaraju's algorithm: (1) Run DFS on original graph G, store vertices by finish time (descending). (2) Reverse graph to get Gᵀ. (3) Run DFS on Gᵀ processing vertices in order from step 1. Each DFS tree in step 3 is one SCC. DFS's property that all vertices in an SCC have finish times within each other's intervals enables this.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between DFS and topological sorting?
flashcards.back: In a DAG, DFS produces a topological sort by outputting vertices in reverse order of their finish times. Theorem: If (u,v) is an edge in DAG, then f[u] > f[v] in any DFS. Thus, taking vertices in decreasing f[] order gives valid topological order. DFS-based topological sort runs in O(V+E) time.
